name: Quantum Mechanics
description: Wave functions, measurement, the Schrödinger equation, and the quantum world. Quantum mechanics describes nature at the atomic and subatomic scales — where particles are waves, measurement disturbs the system, and the act of observation plays a fundamental role. The most precisely tested theory in the history of science.
axioms: State vectors live in Hilbert space; observables are Hermitian operators
Schrödinger equation: iℏ∂ψ/∂t = Ĥψ governs time evolution of quantum states
Born rule: probability of measuring outcome is |⟨φ|ψ⟩|² — the squared amplitude of the wave function
Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2 — certain pairs of observables cannot be simultaneously known
Measurement postulates: measurement collapses the state to an eigenstate of the observable being measured
Identical particles: bosons have symmetric wave functions (integer spin), fermions antisymmetric (half-integer spin)
Pauli exclusion principle: no two fermions can occupy the same quantum state
reasoning_patterns: Eigenvalue decomposition: find energy levels by solving Ĥ|n⟩ = E_n|n⟩ (time-independent Schrödinger equation)
Perturbation theory: approximate corrections to known solutions when a small additional potential is present
Variational method: find an upper bound on the ground state energy using a trial wave function
Density matrix formalism: handle mixed states and decoherence when the system is entangled with an environment
Path integral formulation: sum over all possible paths between initial and final states (Feynman)
Symmetry and group theory: classify states by quantum numbers from irreducible representations of symmetry groups
WKB approximation: semiclassical approximation for slowly varying potentials (tunneling rates)
boundary_conditions: Does not account for relativistic effects — requires quantum field theory for such regimes
The measurement problem (what constitutes a measurement? what causes collapse?) is philosophically unresolved
Quantum gravity remains unsolved — quantum mechanics and general relativity are mathematically incompatible
Decoherence explains the emergence of classicality from quantum systems but does not explain collapse
Bohmian mechanics and many-worlds are interpretations, not experimentally distinguishable
Does not predict its own domain of validity — must assume the classical world to describe the quantum
key_references: Griffiths, D.J. Introduction to Quantum Mechanics
Dirac, P.A.M. The Principles of Quantum Mechanics
Landau & Lifshitz. Quantum Mechanics (Course of Theoretical Physics Vol.3)
Feynman, R. The Feynman Lectures on Physics Vol.3
von Neumann, J. Mathematical Foundations of Quantum Mechanics
canonical_truths: ℏ = h/2π ≈ 1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s is the fundamental quantum of action
Quantum mechanics is the most precisely tested theory in physics — QED matches experiment to 12 decimal places
Bell's theorem: no local hidden variable theory can reproduce all predictions of quantum mechanics
Quantum tunneling is real and essential for processes like nuclear fusion in stars and semiconductor operation
Entanglement is a resource for quantum computing and cryptography, NOT a communication channel (no FTL signaling)
The double-slit experiment exhibits the core quantum mystery: which-path information destroys interference
